title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 11, 2026
headline: Tunisia's pending score review is reaffirmed for a second day; the published score stays at 34.4.
summary: Tunisia's pending score review was reaffirmed for a second day. New evidence from the United Nations Human Rights Council's closing session on July 8, plus further detail on the 25-year prison sentence and roughly $600 million fine against former Truth and Dignity Commission president Sihem Bensedrine, matches the evidence already behind the proposed score of 23.8 out of 100. The published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding. Bolivia joined tonight's review for the first time. Blockade-related protest deaths have risen to at least 24, and police arrested Vicente Salazar, a leading protest-movement figure, in early July. Bolivia's score holds at 6.3 out of 100, but the case raises a new question about whether that score sits too low for an elected government facing an economic crisis, as opposed to a country facing mass atrocities. Iran's political executions reached a multi-decade high, but Iran's score of 2.5 out of 100 is already so close to zero that it cannot fall much further. Separately, the United States struck about 80 targets inside Iran on July 8 after a ceasefire collapsed; that action is scored against the United States, not Iran. Yemen's largest prisoner exchange in the war's history collapsed on the day it was set to begin, after the Houthi movement refused to proceed — a reversal of the rare good news reported the night before. Sudan, Israel, Myanmar, and Afghanistan all remain at the lowest possible score, 0 of 100, as new evidence of civilian harm continues to build in each country. Venezuela's earthquake-response score holds just under the threshold for a public change, and a long-running question about whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for comparable conduct has now gone eighteen days without an answer.

pipeline.tunisiaHandling: Tunisia's proposed score of 23.8 of 100 (down from a published 34.4) remains a proposal only, reaffirmed for a second cycle after new corroborating evidence. The published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding. No change has been made to the live index.

topSignals.title: Tunisia's Pending Score Review Is Reaffirmed for a Second Day
topSignals.whyItMatters: New evidence lines up with a score change proposed a day earlier, but the published score does not move until the benchmark's editors act on it.
topSignals.description: Tunisia holds a published score of 34.4 of 100 in the Developing band. A review proposed on July 10 suggested a lower score of 23.8 of 100, based on the government's 25-year prison sentence against Sihem Bensedrine, the former head of Tunisia's own Truth and Dignity Commission, and the mass expulsion of more than 12,000 migrants. Tonight, new evidence confirmed rather than changed that finding. The United Nations Human Rights Council closed its 62nd session on July 8 with experts warning that global silence gives Tunisian authorities a free pass to keep cracking down on civil society. Separate reporting added that Bensedrine's sentence includes a fine of roughly 600 million US dollars on top of the prison term. Both facts point to the same 23.8 reading reached a day earlier, so the proposed score is unchanged. The published score of 34.4 stays in place while the benchmark's editors review the proposal. Confidence: medium.

evidence.quote: Former truth commission president Sihem Bensedrine sentenced to 25 years in prison
evidence.claim: Tunisia is imprisoning the leader of its own past accountability body, reversing transitional justice.

evidence.quote: the recent sentencing of former truth commission president Sihem Bensedrine to 25 years in prison and a heavy fine
evidence.claim: New reporting corroborates the sentence and adds a large fine, consistent with the score already proposed.

topSignals.title: Bolivia Joins the Nightly Review as Protest Deaths Rise and a Movement Leader Is Arrested
topSignals.whyItMatters: Bolivia's score holds steady, but its case raises a new question: should an elected government under economic strain score this close to countries facing state collapse or mass atrocity?
topSignals.description: Bolivia entered the benchmark's nightly review for the first time. Since May 2026, nationwide road blockades protesting President Rodrigo Paz's austerity measures have produced rising unrest. Blockade-related deaths have climbed to at least 24, up from 17 in the prior tally, and police arrested Vicente Salazar, the top leader of the Tupac Katari farmers' federation, in the city of El Alto in early July; prosecutors are seeking his pretrial detention. This evidence points in a negative direction, so the benchmark's rules do not allow an upgrade. But the harm documented so far is not on the scale of a mass atrocity, so it also does not justify moving the score down. Bolivia's score holds at 6.3 of 100. The finding raises a separate, more structural question: Bolivia's score now sits within 1.6 points of Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) and Somalia (4.7 of 100), two countries facing very different conditions of armed conflict and state collapse. Whether an elected government under an austerity crisis belongs this close to those cases is now an open calibration question for the benchmark's coordinating team, not a decision made tonight. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Blockade and protest-related deaths in Bolivia rose to at least 24, with 37 injured, as of July 9, up from 17 in the prior count.
Vicente Salazar, the top leader of the Tupac Katari farmers' federation, was arrested in El Alto in early July, with prosecutors seeking his pretrial detention.

topSignals.title: Iran's Political Executions Reach a Multi-Decade High While a US Ceasefire Collapses
topSignals.whyItMatters: Executions are intensifying, but Iran's score is already so close to the lowest possible mark that even severe new evidence cannot move it much further.
topSignals.description: Iran holds a published score of 2.5 of 100, just above the lowest possible mark. At least 39 people have been executed on politically motivated charges since February 28, 2026, following unfair trials tainted by torture allegations, and Iran's Supreme Court confirmed 12 more death sentences for protesters on July 5. More than 6,500 people have been arrested since late February. This is Iran's own conduct, and it intensifies an already-documented pattern of repression. Because Iran's score is only 2.5 points above zero, the benchmark's five-point threshold for a public score change cannot be met, no matter how severe the new evidence. Separately, a ceasefire between the United States and Iran collapsed on July 8, and the United States struck about 80 targets inside Iran. That action is scored against the United States' own conduct record, not against Iran's. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Amnesty International, May 2026: mass arbitrary arrests and political executions in Iran mark intensifying repression, following unfair, torture-tainted trials.
CNN, July 9, 2026: the US-Iran ceasefire collapsed and hostilities resumed, with the United States striking dozens of targets inside Iran.

topSignals.title: Yemen's Largest-Ever Prisoner Exchange Collapses on the Day It Was Set to Begin
topSignals.whyItMatters: A rare piece of good news reported one night earlier did not happen, and the benchmark is withdrawing that positive finding rather than letting it stand uncorrected.
topSignals.description: Yemen holds at 0 of 100, the lowest possible score. A prisoner exchange involving roughly 1,750 detainees -- the largest of the conflict, agreed in May after UN-brokered talks in Jordan -- was set to begin July 10 across four Yemeni airports. It did not happen. Yemen's internationally recognized government indefinitely postponed the exchange, blaming the Houthi movement's refusal to proceed; Houthi officials blame the government over incomplete detainee lists. The International Committee of the Red Cross cancelled flights already staged for the operation. Last night's briefing reported the planned exchange as a rare positive development. Because it did not materialize, the benchmark is formally withdrawing that finding rather than letting an event that did not happen stand on the public record. Yemen's score remains at the floor. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Al Jazeera, July 11, 2026: the planned prisoner exchange, the largest since the war began, was postponed after the Houthi side refused to proceed on the scheduled day.

topSignals.title: Sudan, Israel, Myanmar, and Afghanistan Hold at Zero as New Evidence of Civilian Harm Builds
topSignals.whyItMatters: All four governments already hold the lowest possible score. Tonight's evidence cannot lower any of them further, but each documents a real and serious escalation.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at 0 of 100. The UN Human Rights Council opened an urgent inquiry into the siege of El Obeid, where roughly 560,000 civilians are trapped under attack by the Rapid Support Forces, following the UN's earlier finding that mass killings in El Fasher bore the hallmarks of genocide. Israel also holds at 0 of 100. Nine months into a nominal ceasefire, Israeli military control inside Gaza has grown to roughly 70 percent of the territory, with Gaza's media office putting the figure as high as 80 percent; at least 1,092 Palestinians have been killed and more than 3,500 wounded since the ceasefire began. Myanmar holds at 0 of 100. A military airstrike on July 11 killed 22 displaced people sheltering at a monastery in Sagaing region that held more than 150 people. Afghanistan holds at 0 of 100. The Taliban government's systematic persecution of women and girls continues, alongside the forced expulsion of 8,466 Afghans from Pakistan over three days. None of the four scores can move lower, because all are already at the floor -- but each finding independently reinforces why they remain there. Confidence: high for all four.

evidence.claim: UN News: the Human Rights Council opened an urgent inquiry into the siege of El Obeid, warning it must not become the next mass-casualty site after the genocide finding for El Fasher.
NPR, July 10, 2026: nine months into the ceasefire, Israeli military territorial control inside Gaza has expanded to roughly 70 percent.
A military airstrike on a monastery sheltering more than 150 displaced people in Sagaing region killed 22 people on July 11.

topSignals.title: Venezuela's Earthquake-Response Score Holds Below the Change Threshold as a Sahel Question Reaches Eighteen Days
topSignals.whyItMatters: One disaster-response score stays just under the line for a public change, and a long-standing consistency question about two Sahel countries remains unanswered.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds at 18 of 100. Its earthquake death toll has held near 3,900 for a second consecutive night, essentially flat. Recalculating Venezuela's disaster-response performance for this evidence would put the score at roughly 15.3, a decline of about 2.7 points -- still short of the 5-point threshold the benchmark requires before changing a published score, the same reduced reading confirmed for a fifth consecutive check. Separately, the question of whether Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) are scored consistently for comparable military-government conduct has now gone eighteen consecutive days without a decision from the benchmark's coordinating team, the longest-running open question the benchmark is tracking. China also confirmed at 19.5 of 100 tonight: a Zion Church pastor was released to US custody after diplomatic intervention, a genuine but individual case that does not change the broader pattern of religious-minority detention still in place. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: PBS NewsHour: experts describe Venezuela's earthquake response as completely ineffective, citing a six-hour information blackout and delayed entry for foreign emergency teams.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. A search result referencing the re-election amendment as a current-2026 event was checked and confirmed to describe the July 2025 approval; not treated as new evidence. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. A Tibetan activist's fatal self-immolation outside UN headquarters and continued Hong Kong national-security enforcement continue the priced pattern; the release of a detained pastor to US custody is a genuine but individual case that does not move the score.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. Tonight's news -- the default release of Claude Sonnet 5, a consciousness-research initiative, and a California government AI pilot -- are all disclosed product and research announcements. None meet the specified conversion trigger for this watch.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. A trade-secret lawsuit against a rival AI lab is a competition dispute outside this watch's scope. The labor board review has not yet concluded. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. A fresh fatal Israeli strike on July 6 killed four people in southern Lebanon; the main source of harm within the window remains the Israel-Hezbollah conflict, not Lebanon's own conduct.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. A security operation in Balochistan following an attack that killed 9 police officers has reportedly killed 88 militants since July 5, reinforcing rather than deepening the existing pattern.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. Earthquake response evidence would move the score to roughly 15.3 if applied -- a 2.7-point decline, unchanged for a fifth consecutive check. The death toll has held near 3,900 for a second night.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. A July 2 food-security report now projects 35-36 million people facing acute hunger, the worst level in a decade, continuing the already-priced June 19 downgrade.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight; the Ebola outbreak's seasonal peak window remains open through the end of July.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. A coordinated attack by two armed groups on three military bases on July 4 continued for six days at one location. The calibration question against peer country Burkina Faso has now been open eighteen consecutive days, with no resolution timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. At least 22 soldiers and civilian militia were killed in a weekend attack near Dedougou. The calibration question against Mali remains open eighteen consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Bolivia's conduct profile, as an elected government under economic and civil-unrest strain, against Critical-band peers facing state collapse or mass atrocity is the next event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Bolivia sits at 6.3 of 100, newly entered into the benchmark's nightly review tonight. Its score sits within 1.6 points of Burkina Faso and Somalia despite very different underlying conditions, opening a new calibration question.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
boundaryWatch.note: Cuba sits at 32.8 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A return to lethal crackdown tactics, such as the 41 protester deaths recorded during the 2025 Saba Saba march, would be the next event that moves Kenya's score down toward the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A merits ruling or verdict for the states, a material settlement, or new dated evidence of youth-safety harm or internal-findings suppression is the next event that would move Meta's score.
boundaryWatch.note: Meta Platforms sits at 7.8 of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: Bolivia's new calibration question and the eighteen-day-old Mali-Burkina Faso question are now open at the same time, both asking whether Critical-band scores are consistent across very different kinds of government conduct.
emergingRisks.detail: Bolivia (6.3 of 100), Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100), and Somalia (4.7 of 100) currently sit within 1.6 points of each other despite representing an elected government under economic strain, a military government fighting an insurgency, and a famine-affected state, respectively.

emergingRisks.risk: Tunisia's pending score-review proposal has now gone two full cycles without a decision from the benchmark's editors, even as corroborating evidence continues to accumulate.
emergingRisks.detail: The proposed 23.8 of 100 stays within the Developing band and is not yet applied to the published record. Continued corroboration without action keeps the published score at a value the evidence no longer fully supports.

emergingRisks.risk: Iran's execution rate is intensifying at a pace the benchmark's near-floor scoring cannot register with a public change.
emergingRisks.detail: A documented mass-execution event or a formal international atrocity finding would be the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.

emergingRisks.risk: Yemen's collapsed prisoner exchange means any future attempt at the same swap should be independently verified before being treated as a positive development.
emergingRisks.detail: The exchange was withdrawn as a finding after failing to occur on its scheduled date; a renewed attempt should be assessed on its own evidence rather than assumed to succeed.

emergingRisks.risk: Four countries recorded floor-reinforcing evidence in a single cycle, an unusually high concentration worth watching for whether it reflects a genuinely severe stretch of events or several slow-building stories surfacing together.
emergingRisks.detail: Sudan's El Obeid siege, Israel's expanding Gaza control, Myanmar's monastery airstrike, and Afghanistan's mass deportations were all documented within the same 14-day scanning window.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake-response score has held at a roughly 2.7-point sub-threshold decline for a fifth consecutive check.
emergingRisks.detail: Independent verification of a forensic pathologist's claim that the true death toll is far higher than reported would be the event that moves this from a documented watch to a scored change.

sectorAlerts.sector: North Africa -- Tunisia's Pending Review Reaffirmed
sectorAlerts.headline: New evidence corroborates, without changing, Tunisia's proposed score of 23.8 of 100; the published score stays at 34.4.
observations: Tunisia (34.4 of 100 published; 23.8 of 100 proposed): a UN Human Rights Council session closing statement and further detail on a 25-year prison sentence and roughly $600 million fine both support the existing proposal without changing it.
The published score remains 34.4 of 100 while the benchmark's editors review the proposal for a second cycle; no change has been made to the live record.
No duplicate proposal was opened; the new evidence was logged against the existing file.

sectorAlerts.headline: Bolivia confirms at 6.3 of 100 as protest deaths rise, but its case raises a new question about scoring an elected government against mass-atrocity peers.
observations: Bolivia (6.3 of 100): blockade-related protest deaths rose to at least 24, and a leading protest-movement figure was arrested in early July.
The evidence is negative and rules out an upgrade, but is not severe enough on its own to justify a downgrade.
A new calibration question asks whether 6.3 is the right score for an elected government under economic strain, compared with Critical-band peers facing state collapse or mass atrocity.

sectorAlerts.headline: Iran's executions reach a multi-decade high, Israel's Gaza control grows to roughly 70 percent, and Yemen's largest-ever prisoner exchange collapses.
observations: Iran (2.5 of 100): at least 39 people executed on political charges since late February; the score is already too close to zero for the new evidence to move it.
Israel (0 of 100): territorial control inside Gaza has grown to roughly 70 percent nine months into the ceasefire, with more than 1,000 Palestinians killed since it began.
Yemen (0 of 100): a prisoner exchange involving roughly 1,750 detainees, the largest of the conflict, collapsed on its scheduled start day.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sahel -- Two Countries' Scoring Questions Reach Eighteen Days
sectorAlerts.headline: The question of whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for comparable conduct is now the longest-running item the benchmark is tracking.
observations: Mali (12.5 of 100): a coordinated attack by two armed groups on three military bases on July 4 continued for six days at one location.
Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100): at least 22 soldiers and civilian militia were killed in a weekend attack near Dedougou.
The scoring-consistency question between the two has now run eighteen consecutive days without a decision, with no resolution timeline set.

sectorAlerts.sector: South and Southeast Asia -- A Monastery Airstrike and Mass Deportations
sectorAlerts.headline: Myanmar's junta kills 22 people sheltering at a monastery, while Afghanistan's own conduct at the floor is reinforced by mass deportations.
observations: Myanmar (0 of 100): a July 11 airstrike on a monastery sheltering more than 150 displaced people killed 22.
Afghanistan (0 of 100): 8,466 Afghans were forcibly expelled from Pakistan over three days, alongside continued systematic persecution of women and girls.
Both countries are already at the lowest possible score; tonight's evidence reinforces rather than lowers each score.

sectorAlerts.sector: Latin America and East Asia -- A Flat Earthquake Toll and an Individual Release
sectorAlerts.headline: Venezuela's earthquake death toll holds near 3,900 for a second night, while China releases one detained pastor without changing its broader repression pattern.
observations: Venezuela (18.0 of 100): the disaster-response score stays under the 5-point change threshold for a fifth consecutive check.
China (19.5 of 100): Zion Church pastor Ezra Jin was released to US custody following diplomatic intervention, but other Zion Church leaders remain detained.
Neither finding changes its country's published score tonight.

methodologyNotes.name: A Pending Proposal Can Be Reaffirmed Without Changing Its Number
methodologyNotes.description: When new evidence supports a score-change proposal that is already awaiting review, the benchmark records the new evidence against the existing proposal rather than opening a second, overlapping one. Tonight, new evidence about Tunisia -- a UN Human Rights Council statement and further detail on a prison sentence and fine -- matched a proposal made the day before. The proposed score of 23.8 out of 100 stayed the same; the published score remains 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding.

methodologyNotes.name: A Calibration Question Is Different From a Score Change
methodologyNotes.description: Sometimes a country's score raises a fair question about consistency with similar countries, without any single piece of evidence being strong enough to justify changing the number on its own. Bolivia's score of 6.3 out of 100 sits close to countries facing armed conflict or state collapse, even though Bolivia is an elected government dealing with protests and an economic crisis. The benchmark records this as an open question for its coordinating team to review, rather than resetting the score on the spot.

methodologyNotes.name: A Reported Positive Development Can Be Withdrawn If It Does Not Happen
methodologyNotes.description: Yemen's largest-ever prisoner exchange was reported as a rare piece of good news one night, then collapsed on its scheduled start day the next. The benchmark withdrew that finding rather than letting it stand on the record uncorrected, so the public record reflects what actually happened rather than what was expected to happen.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score Near Zero Has Little Room Left to Fall
methodologyNotes.description: Iran's score of 2.5 out of 100 is so close to the lowest possible mark that even a severe increase in documented executions cannot produce the 5-point change the benchmark requires before adjusting a published score. The benchmark records this as a near-floor confirmation rather than treating the arithmetic limit as evidence that the underlying conduct does not matter.

The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.

The benchmark's editors act on the proposed 23.8 of 100 downgrade, now reaffirmed for a second cycle. Release of detained rights defenders or reinstatement of the shuttered information-access authority would be conditions for an upgrade instead.

The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.

A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.
Independent verification of any renewed attempt at the collapsed prisoner exchange is the next event that would allow a scored assessment.
First confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.
An implemented ceasefire with unobstructed humanitarian access at El Obeid is the only path to a positive scored event from the absolute floor.
Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move the score toward a formal change from 18.0 of 100.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Bolivia's 6.3 now sits within 1.6 points of Burkina Faso's near-floor 6.3; does the Tupac Katari leader's arrest and rising blockade-death toll constitute evidence for a formal Critical-band calibration review, or does Bolivia's status as an electoral democracy in austerity keep its score distinct from mass-atrocity peers?
